title: Pasta alla Norma
subtitle: Spaghetti with aubergine, tomato, and ricotta salata
excerpt: Fried aubergine tossed with spaghetti in a garlicky tomato sauce and finished with dried ricotta and basil. Sicily's most celebrated pasta — substantial, sweet from the aubergine, and exactly as straightforward as it looks.
sourceNotes: Based on the Sicilian pasta alla Norma tradition as documented in Mary Taylor Simeti, 'Pomp and Sustenance: Twenty-Five Centuries of Sicilian Food' (1989) and Anna Tasca Lanza, 'The Heart of Sicily' (1993). The dish is named after Bellini's opera Norma, composed in Catania.

content.text: Pasta alla Norma comes from Catania, in eastern Sicily, where it is considered the defining local dish. The aubergine is fried in olive oil until golden and slightly collapsed before it goes into the sauce — this frying step is not optional. Raw aubergine added directly to tomato sauce never reaches the right texture: soft, yielding, with a slight caramel edge. Ricotta salata, the dried, salted ricotta that finishes the dish, has a sharper, firmer character than fresh ricotta. Pecorino is a reasonable substitute if ricotta salata is unavailable.

items.prepNote: plus extra for frying the aubergine
dried ricotta salata, grated; or pecorino as substitute

content.text: Heat a generous pour of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium-high heat. Fry {{aubergine}} of aubergine cubes in batches until golden on all sides, about 4–5 minutes per batch. Set aside on kitchen paper. The aubergine should absorb the oil and colour; do not crowd the pan or it will steam instead of fry.
Make the sauce and cook the pasta

In the same pan with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, fry {{garlic}} sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli until fragrant, about 1 minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, crushing them with a spoon. Season and simmer for 10 minutes. Meanwhile, cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti in a large pot of well-salted water until al dente. Reserve a mug of pasta water before draining.

Add the fried aubergine and most of the {{basil}} basil leaves to the tomato sauce. Drain the pasta and toss through the sauce with a splash of pasta water. Divide between bowls, scatter the remaining basil, and finish with grated {{ricotta}} ricotta salata or pecorino.

items.symptom: Aubergine is greasy and soft rather than golden
items.cause: Pan overcrowded or heat too low
items.fix: Fry in small batches with enough oil. Aubergine absorbs oil rapidly at first, then releases it once properly cooked.
Add a small pinch of sugar and cook for another 5 minutes.

content.text: The story goes that pasta alla Norma acquired its name when the Catanese playwright and sometime cook Nino Martoglio tasted it and declared it as great as Bellini's opera. Whether or not that is true, the dish is distinctly Catanian — it appears in the city's home kitchens and restaurants in a form that has changed very little for at least a century. Outside Sicily, it is one of the most prepared of all southern Italian pasta dishes, partly because its ingredients are pantry staples for most cooks.
